articles.title: Why this VC is choosing Indian space startups over SpaceX
articles.description: Vishesh Rajaram, Founding Partner at Speciale Invest, an early-stage VC fund in India, says SpaceX has done for space what Amazon did for cloud computing. He says he is bullish on India’s space-tech startup ecosystem, citing strong innovation and supportive policy moves aimed at opening up and privatizing the sector.

articles.title: Study supports atropine drops for managing childhood short-sightedness
articles.description: Low concentration atropine eye drops are a safe and effective treatment for short-sightedness (myopia) in UK children, although the effects are small, suggests a clinical trial published by The BMJ today.

articles.title: ChatGPT fills gaps like a confabulator, not a hallucinating mind
articles.description: A Perspective article argues that large language model errors more closely resemble confabulations, while speech recognition errors, such as unsupported Whisper transcriptions, are functionally closer to hallucinations. The authors stress that these parallels are behavioral and provisional, not evidence of shared consciousness, and suggest that psychiatric concepts could help improve AI reliability.

articles.title: Scientists uncover protein-folding mechanism linked to diabetes progression
articles.description: Origami masters turn simple sheets of paper into ornate sculptures. In the origami of life, our cells must fold proteins into specific three-dimensional shapes before they can carry out their biological jobs.

articles.title: Thai king's eldest daughter dies after long illness, royal palace says
articles.description: Princess Bajrakitiyabha Narendira Debyavati, who had spent the past three and a half years in a coma due to a heart condition, is being remembered for her efforts to improve the livelihoods of female prisoners and her diplomatic career.

articles.title: Childhood lead exposure declines but disparities persist nationwide
articles.description: Blood lead levels in young children in the United States declined in the last decade, but state data reveal that children of color and children from low-wealth families are still exposed to higher levels of lead, according to a new study in the American Journal of Public Health.

articles.title: 5 free cozy mobile games that prove relaxing doesn't have to cost a thing

articles.title: Mesothelioma remains deadly despite declining asbestos exposure rates
articles.description: Mesothelioma deaths and diagnoses continue to rise in the United States despite decades of asbestos regulation and reduced industrial use, according to a new national analysis from Sylvester Comprehensive Cancer Center, part of the University of Miami Miller School of Medicine.

articles.title: The cerebellum may help aging brains stay sharper
articles.description: A large MRI study of more than 47,000 adults found that cerebellar aging is uneven, with posterior association regions and some motor-related areas showing steeper age-related changes. Larger cerebellar volumes were linked to better cognitive performance, supporting a threshold-reserve model in which the cerebellum may help sustain cognition before Alzheimer’s pathology becomes widespread.

articles.title: The midlife habits that could make or break your brain health long-term
articles.description: Researchers increasingly believe the 40s, 50s and 60s represent a critical window for protecting cognitive health later in life.

articles.title: Gut bacterial molecule reduces intestinal inflammation by slowing cell energy
